---
title: "Foundations of Medical Informatics"
lang: en-GB
---

## What is Medical Informatics {#sec-mi-definition}

### Definition and disciplinary scope

Medical Informatics is a scientific discipline at the intersection of
three knowledge domains: Information Science, Computer Science and
Health Science [@Haux2006]. Its object of study focuses on how
information is generated, organised, stored, retrieved and used in the
clinical and organisational context of healthcare.

There is a frequent tendency to reduce Medical Informatics to the
mere application of computing tools in healthcare. This perspective
is, however, reductive: it is a discipline that integrates technology,
information management and clinical practice in an inseparable way,
requiring simultaneous competence in all three constituent domains
[@Collen2015].

### The three foundational pillars

Three structural pillars underpin the discipline:

**Information** — encompasses models for representing clinical
knowledge, data semantics and its formal structuring. The quality of
clinical information is decisive for patient safety and continuity of
care.

**Computing** — comprises information systems, network architectures
and computational models that support the processing and communication
of clinical data. This pillar provides the technical means without
which information sharing between systems would be impossible.

**Health** — encompasses clinical practice, care processes and the
ethical and legal responsibilities arising from the use of information
systems in the context of patient care. No technological solution can
be designed without a solid understanding of the clinical domain it
aims to support.

The following figure illustrates the position of Medical Informatics
as the intersection area of the three domains:

```{=html}
<!--
  Venn diagram — three pillars of Medical Informatics
  Static SVG, teal palette.
-->
<div style="display:flex;justify-content:center;margin:2rem 0;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 420 360" width="380" height="330"
     xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
     aria-label="Venn diagram — three pillars of Medical Informatics">
  <defs>
    <radialGradient id="gInfo" cx="50%" cy="50%" r="50%">
      <stop offset="0%" stop-color="#0d7c8c" stop-opacity="0.50"/>
      <stop offset="100%" stop-color="#0d7c8c" stop-opacity="0.15"/>
    </radialGradient>
    <radialGradient id="gComp" cx="50%" cy="50%" r="50%">
      <stop offset="0%" stop-color="#1ab3a6" stop-opacity="0.50"/>
      <stop offset="100%" stop-color="#1ab3a6" stop-opacity="0.15"/>
    </radialGradient>
    <radialGradient id="gSau" cx="50%" cy="50%" r="50%">
      <stop offset="0%" stop-color="#0a5c6b" stop-opacity="0.50"/>
      <stop offset="100%" stop-color="#0a5c6b" stop-opacity="0.15"/>
    </radialGradient>
  </defs>
  <circle cx="168" cy="148" r="108" fill="url(#gInfo)" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <circle cx="252" cy="148" r="108" fill="url(#gComp)" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <circle cx="210" cy="220" r="108" fill="url(#gSau)" stroke="#0a5c6b" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <text x="112" y="96" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11.5" font-weight="600"
        fill="#0a3d4a" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif">Information</text>
  <text x="112" y="111" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11.5" font-weight="600"
        fill="#0a3d4a" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif">Science</text>
  <text x="308" y="96" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11.5" font-weight="600"
        fill="#0a3d4a" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif">Computer</text>
  <text x="308" y="111" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11.5" font-weight="600"
        fill="#0a3d4a" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif">Science</text>
  <text x="210" y="322" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11.5" font-weight="600"
        fill="#0a3d4a" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif">Health Science</text>
  <text x="210" y="192" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="700"
        fill="#fff" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif">Medical</text>
  <text x="210" y="208" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="700"
        fill="#fff" font-family="system-ui,sans-serif">Informatics</text>
</svg>
</div>
```

### Differences from conventional informatics

Medical Informatics differs from general-purpose informatics through
a set of characteristics that impose additional requirements for
rigour, safety and accountability:

- **Sensitive and critical data** — clinical data are, by nature,
  special-category personal data subject to specific legal frameworks
  (notably the General Data Protection Regulation and sectoral health
  legislation).

- **Direct clinical impact** — errors in recording, transmitting or
  interpreting clinical information can have direct consequences for
  patient health and safety.

- **Demanding regulation** — health information systems are subject to
  technical standards and legal requirements with no parallel in other
  sectors.

- **High organisational complexity** — hospitals and health centres are
  highly complex, multidisciplinary, multi-service organisations, which
  poses particular challenges for the integration and governance of
  information systems.

## Historical Evolution {#sec-evolution}

### From hospital administration to digital health

The history of Medical Informatics can be traced across four major
periods, each marked by a technological transformation that profoundly
altered how clinical information is managed.

**1960s–70s: first hospital administrative systems.**
The first computing systems introduced in hospitals were essentially
administrative in scope: admission management, bed control and billing
processing. The clinical record remained, almost entirely, on paper.

**1980s–90s: clinical computerisation and digital imaging.**
This period was marked by the progressive introduction of systems
supporting clinical activity and, particularly significantly, by the
development of the DICOM standard (*Digital Imaging and Communications
in Medicine*), which normalised the storage and transmission of digital
medical images, establishing the technological foundation for modern
PACS systems.

**2000s: HL7 integration and electronic records.**
The first decade of the century saw the widespread adoption of electronic
clinical records and the adoption of the HL7 (*Health Level Seven*)
standard as the integration language between hospital systems.
Technical interoperability between different applications became an
operational requirement, not merely an aspiration.

**Today: FHIR APIs, interoperability and digital health.**
The current paradigm rests on the FHIR standard (*Fast Healthcare
Interoperability Resources*), which replaces the classical HL7 messaging
model with a REST API architecture, making integration more agile and
aligned with modern web standards. There is simultaneously growing
pressure to create European-scale health data spaces, materialised in
the EHDS regulation [@EHDS2025].

The following timeline summarises the four key milestones:

```{=html}
<!--
  Horizontal timeline — historical evolution of Medical Informatics.
  Four stages with teal accent. Responsive via flexbox.
-->
<div style="overflow-x:auto;margin:2rem 0;">
<div style="display:flex;align-items:flex-start;min-width:560px;
            padding:0 1rem;gap:0;position:relative;">
  <div style="position:absolute;top:28px;left:calc(12.5% + 20px);
              right:calc(12.5% + 20px);height:3px;
              background:linear-gradient(90deg,#0d7c8c,#1ab3a6);
              z-index:0;"></div>
  <div style="flex:1;display:flex;flex-direction:column;align-items:center;
              position:relative;z-index:1;">
    <div style="width:56px;height:56px;border-radius:50%;
                background:#0d7c8c;color:#fff;display:flex;
                align-items:center;justify-content:center;
                font-size:1.3rem;font-weight:700;
                box-shadow:0 2px 8px rgba(13,124,140,0.35);">1</div>
    <div style="margin-top:.7rem;text-align:center;font-size:.82rem;
                font-weight:700;color:#0a3d4a;">1960s–70s</div>
    <div style="font-size:.75rem;color:#444;text-align:center;
                margin-top:.3rem;max-width:90px;">Hospital administrative systems</div>
  </div>
  <div style="flex:1;display:flex;flex-direction:column;align-items:center;
              position:relative;z-index:1;">
    <div style="width:56px;height:56px;border-radius:50%;
                background:#0b8f9e;color:#fff;display:flex;
                align-items:center;justify-content:center;
                font-size:1.3rem;font-weight:700;
                box-shadow:0 2px 8px rgba(13,124,140,0.30);">2</div>
    <div style="margin-top:.7rem;text-align:center;font-size:.82rem;
                font-weight:700;color:#0a3d4a;">1980s–90s</div>
    <div style="font-size:.75rem;color:#444;text-align:center;
                margin-top:.3rem;max-width:90px;">Clinical computerisation and DICOM</div>
  </div>
  <div style="flex:1;display:flex;flex-direction:column;align-items:center;
              position:relative;z-index:1;">
    <div style="width:56px;height:56px;border-radius:50%;
                background:#0fa2ae;color:#fff;display:flex;
                align-items:center;justify-content:center;
                font-size:1.3rem;font-weight:700;
                box-shadow:0 2px 8px rgba(26,179,166,0.30);">3</div>
    <div style="margin-top:.7rem;text-align:center;font-size:.82rem;
                font-weight:700;color:#0a3d4a;">2000s</div>
    <div style="font-size:.75rem;color:#444;text-align:center;
                margin-top:.3rem;max-width:90px;">HL7 integration and electronic records</div>
  </div>
  <div style="flex:1;display:flex;flex-direction:column;align-items:center;
              position:relative;z-index:1;">
    <div style="width:56px;height:56px;border-radius:50%;
                background:#1ab3a6;color:#fff;display:flex;
                align-items:center;justify-content:center;
                font-size:1.3rem;font-weight:700;
                box-shadow:0 2px 8px rgba(26,179,166,0.35);">4</div>
    <div style="margin-top:.7rem;text-align:center;font-size:.82rem;
                font-weight:700;color:#0a3d4a;">Today</div>
    <div style="font-size:.75rem;color:#444;text-align:center;
                margin-top:.3rem;max-width:90px;">FHIR APIs and digital health</div>
  </div>
</div>
</div>
```

::: {.callout-note}
**To integrate is to connect. To interoperate is to understand.**
The distinction between technical integration and semantic
interoperability is one of the central conceptual axes of this course.
:::

## The Hospital as a Sociotechnical System {#sec-sociotechnical}

### Technology, people and process dimensions

The modern hospital is not simply a healthcare organisation — it is a
complex sociotechnical system in which technology, people and processes
articulate in an inseparable way. No information system creates value
if it is designed or implemented in isolation, without considering the
other two vertices of this triangle.

The **technological** dimension encompasses the information systems
themselves, the network infrastructure that connects them and the
medical devices that generate and consume clinical data. Technology is
a necessary but not sufficient condition for improving healthcare.

The **people** dimension comprises health professionals (physicians,
nurses, technicians), hospital managers and administrators, and the
patients and their carers. People produce, validate and use clinical
information; they also bear the consequences of any failures in the
systems.

The **process** dimension encompasses the clinical workflows that
structure care activity, protocols and best-practice guidelines, and
the inter-service coordination mechanisms. The quality of processes is
frequently the determining factor for the success or failure of a
hospital computerisation project.

### Hospital complexity

The particular complexity of hospitals results from three structural
characteristics:

- **Multidisciplinary nature** — different clinical specialties, each
  with their own language, processes and organisational culture, coexist
  within the same institution and share information about the same
  patients.

- **Multi-service nature** — each hospital service holds a significant
  degree of functional and organisational autonomy, which complicates
  the adoption of uniform solutions and centralised information
  governance.

- **Multiplicity of systems** — a medium-sized hospital typically runs
  dozens of specialist information systems (central hospital system,
  medical imaging system, laboratory system, operating theatre system,
  among others), each with its own data models, interfaces and vendors.

### Clinical versus administrative data

In the hospital context, two broad types of data can be distinguished,
with different natures and requirements:

| Type | Examples | Requirements |
|------|----------|-------------|
| **Clinical** | Diagnoses, prescriptions, test results, clinical observations | Precision, confidentiality, temporal integrity |
| **Administrative** | Patient identification, scheduling, billing, episodes | Consistency, uniqueness, availability |

: Types of data in a hospital context {#tbl-data}

The distinct nature of these two data types implies equally distinct
requirements for modelling, protection and governance. Clinical data
demand a degree of semantic precision that administrative data alone
do not require; the latter are frequently the linking key that allows
clinical information dispersed across multiple systems to be aggregated.

## Health Information Systems {#sec-his-general}

### Definition of an information system

An information system is defined as an organised set of people,
processes and technology that collects, stores, processes and
distributes information with the objective of supporting decision-making
in an organisation [@Haux2006]. This tripartite definition — input,
processing, output — applies both to generic information systems and
to systems specifically designed for the healthcare context.

In the hospital domain, the input corresponds to the collection of
clinical and administrative data generated throughout the patient's
journey; processing involves storing, validating and structuring those
data; and the output materialises as information available to support
clinical, managerial and statistical decisions.

### The data–information–knowledge chain

Three levels can be distinguished in the hierarchy of clinical
information, with direct implications for the design and evaluation of
systems:

**Data** are isolated records without context: a numeric value, a date,
a code. On their own, data have no operational clinical meaning.

**Information** results from contextualising data: a numeric value
associated with a physiological parameter, a patient and a point in
time becomes information with clinical utility.

**Knowledge** emerges when information is interpreted in the light of
clinical models and accumulated experience, enabling a diagnostic or
therapeutic decision to be justified.

Medical Informatics is not merely concerned with storing data, but with
transforming them into knowledge useful for clinical practice.

The following diagram illustrates this progression using a blood glucose
record as an example:

```{=html}
<!--
  Data → Information → Knowledge chain
  Concrete example: blood glucose record.
-->
<div style="display:flex;flex-wrap:wrap;gap:0;
            align-items:stretch;margin:2rem 0;justify-content:center;">

  <!-- DATA -->
  <div style="flex:1;min-width:150px;max-width:200px;
              background:#f0fafa;border:2px solid #0d7c8c;
              border-radius:8px 0 0 8px;padding:1.2rem 1rem;
              display:flex;flex-direction:column;align-items:center;">
    <div style="font-size:.7rem;font-weight:700;letter-spacing:.08em;
                text-transform:uppercase;color:#0d7c8c;margin-bottom:.5rem;">
      Data
    </div>
    <div style="font-size:1.6rem;font-weight:800;color:#0a3d4a;">
      210
    </div>
    <div style="font-size:.75rem;color:#555;margin-top:.3rem;
                text-align:center;">
      Isolated numeric value.<br>No context.
    </div>
  </div>

  <!-- arrow 1 -->
  <div style="display:flex;align-items:center;padding:0 .3rem;">
    <svg width="32" height="24" viewBox="0 0 32 24"
         xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
      <path d="M2 12 H26 M20 5 L28 12 L20 19"
            stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="2.5"
            fill="none" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"/>
    </svg>
  </div>

  <!-- INFORMATION -->
  <div style="flex:1;min-width:160px;max-width:210px;
              background:#e6f7f6;border:2px solid #1ab3a6;
              border-radius:0;padding:1.2rem 1rem;
              display:flex;flex-direction:column;align-items:center;">
    <div style="font-size:.7rem;font-weight:700;letter-spacing:.08em;
                text-transform:uppercase;color:#0d7c8c;margin-bottom:.5rem;">
      Information
    </div>
    <div style="font-size:.9rem;font-weight:700;color:#0a3d4a;
                text-align:center;">
      Glycaemia = 210 mg/dL<br>
      <span style="font-weight:400;font-size:.8rem;">
        John Silva · 14:32 · fasting
      </span>
    </div>
    <div style="font-size:.75rem;color:#555;margin-top:.5rem;
                text-align:center;">
      Contextualised data:<br>who, what, when.
    </div>
  </div>

  <!-- arrow 2 -->
  <div style="display:flex;align-items:center;padding:0 .3rem;">
    <svg width="32" height="24" viewBox="0 0 32 24"
         xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
      <path d="M2 12 H26 M20 5 L28 12 L20 19"
            stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="2.5"
            fill="none" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"/>
    </svg>
  </div>

  <!-- KNOWLEDGE -->
  <div style="flex:1;min-width:160px;max-width:220px;
              background:#d6f2ef;border:2px solid #0a5c6b;
              border-radius:0 8px 8px 0;padding:1.2rem 1rem;
              display:flex;flex-direction:column;align-items:center;">
    <div style="font-size:.7rem;font-weight:700;letter-spacing:.08em;
                text-transform:uppercase;color:#0a5c6b;margin-bottom:.5rem;">
      Knowledge
    </div>
    <div style="font-size:.85rem;font-weight:700;color:#0a3d4a;
                text-align:center;">
      Fasting hyperglycaemia.<br>
      <span style="font-weight:400;font-size:.8rem;">
        ADA diagnostic criterion: ≥ 126 mg/dL.<br>
        Evaluate T2DM.
      </span>
    </div>
    <div style="font-size:.75rem;color:#555;margin-top:.5rem;
                text-align:center;">
      Decision grounded<br>in clinical models.
    </div>
  </div>

</div>
```

### Organisational flows and the structural role of IS

Clinical information does not exist in isolated silos: it circulates
continuously between services, between professionals and between
institutions. From this circulation depends the coordination between
the different actors in the health system; from coordination depends
continuity of care; and from continuity of care depends, ultimately,
patient safety.

This establishes a logical chain with direct implications for the
governance of hospital information systems:

> *No information systems → no coordination → no continuity of care →
> increased clinical risk.*

This chain demonstrates that information systems are not, in modern
hospitals, a technological convenience: they are critical infrastructure.
Their unavailability or degradation has direct effects on the quality
and safety of care provided.

## Interoperability {#sec-interop}

### Three levels of interoperability

Interoperability is the ability of different systems to exchange
information and to use that information effectively [@BensonGrieve2021].
In the healthcare context, three complementary levels can be identified:

**Technical interoperability** — systems can communicate at the level
of transport, format and protocol. It is a necessary but not sufficient
condition: it guarantees that data reach the destination, not that they
are correctly interpreted.

**Semantic interoperability** — systems share the meaning of the data
exchanged. For two systems to "understand each other", they must use
controlled vocabularies and standardised terminologies (such as
SNOMED CT, LOINC or ICD), so that the same clinical concept has the
same meaning in both systems.

**Organisational interoperability** — processes, policies and the
legal framework are compatible between organisations wishing to share
information. This level is frequently the most difficult to achieve,
as it requires governance agreements that go beyond the purely technical
dimension.

The following diagram positions the three levels hierarchically,
showing that each higher level presupposes the one below:

```{=html}
<!--
  Interoperability pyramid — three stacked levels.
  Technical (base) → Semantic → Organisational (top).
-->
<div style="display:flex;flex-direction:column;align-items:center;
            margin:2rem 0;gap:4px;">

  <!-- Level 3 — Organisational (top) -->
  <div style="width:260px;background:#0a3d4a;color:#fff;
              border-radius:6px;padding:.8rem 1rem;text-align:center;
              box-shadow:0 2px 8px rgba(0,0,0,0.12);">
    <div style="font-size:.7rem;font-weight:700;letter-spacing:.06em;
                text-transform:uppercase;opacity:.75;margin-bottom:.25rem;">
      Level 3
    </div>
    <div style="font-size:.95rem;font-weight:700;">Organisational</div>
    <div style="font-size:.75rem;opacity:.85;margin-top:.2rem;">
      Policies, governance, legal framework
    </div>
  </div>

  <div style="color:#0a5c6b;font-size:1.2rem;line-height:1;">▼</div>

  <!-- Level 2 — Semantic -->
  <div style="width:340px;background:#0d7c8c;color:#fff;
              border-radius:6px;padding:.8rem 1rem;text-align:center;
              box-shadow:0 2px 8px rgba(13,124,140,0.20);">
    <div style="font-size:.7rem;font-weight:700;letter-spacing:.06em;
                text-transform:uppercase;opacity:.75;margin-bottom:.25rem;">
      Level 2
    </div>
    <div style="font-size:.95rem;font-weight:700;">Semantic</div>
    <div style="font-size:.75rem;opacity:.85;margin-top:.2rem;">
      Controlled vocabularies, standardised terminologies
    </div>
  </div>

  <div style="color:#0d7c8c;font-size:1.2rem;line-height:1;">▼</div>

  <!-- Level 1 — Technical (base) -->
  <div style="width:420px;background:#1ab3a6;color:#fff;
              border-radius:6px;padding:.8rem 1rem;text-align:center;
              box-shadow:0 2px 8px rgba(26,179,166,0.20);">
    <div style="font-size:.7rem;font-weight:700;letter-spacing:.06em;
                text-transform:uppercase;opacity:.75;margin-bottom:.25rem;">
      Level 1
    </div>
    <div style="font-size:.95rem;font-weight:700;">Technical</div>
    <div style="font-size:.75rem;opacity:.85;margin-top:.2rem;">
      Transport, format, protocol (HL7, FHIR, TCP/IP)
    </div>
  </div>

</div>
```

::: {.callout-tip}
**Illustrative example**

Two hospitals can exchange messages (technical interoperability).
One records the diagnosis as "Arterial hypertension"; the other uses
the abbreviation "HTN". The systems communicate — but do they
understand each other? No, in the absence of shared standardised
terminologies. This example illustrates the difference between
technical and semantic interoperability.
:::

### The problem of information fragmentation

One of the main obstacles to interoperability in current health
systems is the fragmentation of clinical information: the same patient
may have records distributed across multiple systems, in multiple
institutions, without any effective aggregation and sharing mechanism.

The practical consequences of this fragmentation are well documented
[@Haggerty2003]:

- unnecessary repetition of examinations due to unavailability of
  previous results;
- delays in clinical decision-making due to unavailability of relevant
  information at the point of care;
- increased clinical risk associated with medication errors, unrecorded
  allergies or contradictory diagnoses across institutions.

### Current challenges

The main challenges facing Medical Informatics today can be grouped
into four vectors:

- **Fragmentation** — clinical information remains dispersed across
  heterogeneous systems, without effective interoperability between
  them.

- **Legacy systems** — many hospitals operate with systems installed
  decades ago, with monolithic architectures and limited capacity to
  integrate with modern technologies.

- **Security and data protection** — the increasing digitalisation of
  clinical data expands the attack surface and requires continuous
  investment in cybersecurity and regulatory compliance.

- **Technological sustainability** — the accelerating evolution of
  information technologies imposes update cycles that health
  organisations, with limited resources, struggle to keep pace with.

## Summary {#sec-summary-01}

This chapter established the conceptual foundations on which Medical
Informatics rests as a scientific discipline. The concepts introduced —
sociotechnical system, data–information–knowledge chain, levels of
interoperability — will be revisited and deepened as technical standards
and integration architectures are introduced.

The following key points are retained:

1. Medical Informatics is an intersection discipline that cannot be
   reduced to any one of its three constituent domains in isolation.
2. The hospital is a sociotechnical system: technology only produces
   value when aligned with clinical processes and organisational
   practices.
3. Interoperability has three levels — technical, semantic and
   organisational — and the absence of any one of them compromises
   continuity of care.
4. The fragmentation of clinical information is the main obstacle to
   health system integration and has direct consequences for patient
   safety.

## Consolidation exercises {.unnumbered}

1. For each of the three pillars of Medical Informatics (Information,
   Computing, Health), identify a concrete problem that cannot be
   solved without simultaneous knowledge of the other two pillars.

2. A hospital decides to replace its clinical record system with a new
   application. What factors, beyond the technical, should be
   considered in this process? Structure the answer using the
   sociotechnical system model.

3. Explain, with a concrete example, why technical interoperability is
   a necessary but not sufficient condition for continuity of care
   between institutions.

4. Considering the logical chain *no IS → no coordination → no
   continuity → increased clinical risk*, identify a real or plausible
   scenario in which a hospital information system failure had or could
   have had direct clinical consequences.
